category_summary:
**Anthropic** dominated the day with a major wave of **Claude Code** updates: a new ['effort max' reasoning mode](/?date=2026-03-15&category=social#item-090d56f0a4ad) using unlimited tokens, voice mode [reaching 100% rollout](/?date=2026-03-15&category=social#item-30fcbbc09bfd), Remote Control session launching, and a "Spring Break" promotion [doubling usage](/?date=2026-03-15&category=social#item-8c871e514dc5) during off-peak hours through March 27. **Boris Cherny**, **trq212**, and CPO **Mike Krieger** all amplified the announcements.

- **Greg Brockman** (OpenAI) [shared the viral story](/?date=2026-03-15&category=social#item-3c9fc8b111e3) of a non-scientist using **ChatGPT** and **AlphaFold** to design a custom mRNA cancer vaccine for his dog, sparking bioethics debate led by **Jeremy Howard** on access to experimental treatments for terminally ill patients
- **Ethan Mollick** [published an original visualization](/?date=2026-03-15&category=social#item-2f7e0fd4f55c) of the AI race via the **GPQA Diamond** benchmark, showing a current three-way tie between **OpenAI**, **Anthropic**, and **Google** with benchmark saturation concerns
- **François Chollet** argued that the [persisting importance of prompt engineering](/?date=2026-03-15&category=social#item-d5307b9b56cb) is strong evidence we remain far from AGI, defining intelligence as rate-of-learning rather than task performance
- **Eliezer Yudkowsky** [proposed a detailed experiment](/?date=2026-03-15&category=social#item-b8c9a9e30c18) to test whether AI self-awareness behaviors emerge from training data contamination, drawing significant engagement
- **Neel Nanda** [highlighted 'out-of-context reasoning'](/?date=2026-03-15&category=social#item-7f6e6b83c38e) as a key mechanistic interpretability finding, while **Mollick** [offered a contrarian take](/?date=2026-03-15&category=social#item-baf0f5fcf10b) that most AI VC bets are implicitly wagers against frontier lab dominance

items.content:
@AISavvyCapital @kevinrose Here's the script that @blevlabs' system generated for me:

X ALIGNED AI INTELLIGENCE BRIEFING — Friday, March 13, 2026, 2:30 PM PST
Source: 63 curated X lists (8,200+ accounts), semantic search across 6,000+ indexed posts.
Produced by Levangie Labs for Robert Scoble's X Aligned.

=== STORY 1: CLAUDE GETS A MILLION TOKENS ===
Anthropic made Claude's 1 million token context window generally available for Claude Opus 4.6 and Sonnet 4.6 at standard pricing — no price increase. This got 14,262 likes and 966 retweets, making it the most-engaged AI announcement of the day. A million tokens is roughly 750,000 words — that's about 10 full novels or an entire codebase. Eric Buess pointed out that context window size isn't the relevant metric — it's the size usable with high integrity. But at standard pricing, this is a massive competitive move. Previously, large context windows required premium tiers. Anthropic just made it free.

=== STORY 2: TRAVIS KALANICK RETURNS WITH ATOMS ===
The founder of Uber is back. Travis Kalanick launched Atoms, a robotics company building "a wheelbase for robots" and "more efficient mines for Earth's industries." Balaji Srinivasan called him "one of the greatest entrepreneurs of all time." Aaron Levie tweeted "lettttts gooooo." This is significant because Kalanick built the world's largest ride-sharing network, and now he's applying that same operational intensity to physical AI and robotics. The "wheelbase for robots" concept suggests a platform play — a standardized mobility base that different robot applications can be built on top of.

=== STORY 3: GOOGLE MAPS GETS A BRAIN ===
Google shipped the biggest Maps upgrade in a decade. "Ask Maps" brings Gemini-powered conversational AI to 2 billion users. You can now ask complex real-world questions like "find me a restaurant with outdoor seating near the park that's good for kids" and get intelligent answers. This is significant because it puts AI in the hands of 2 billion people who use Google Maps daily — the largest AI distribution event since ChatGPT launched. Immersive navigation also redesigns the driving experience.

=== STORY 4: DIGG DIES AGAIN — AI BOT SPAM KILLED IT ===
Digg announced a "hard reset" and shut down, just two months after Kevin Rose and Alexis Ohanian relaunched it. The reason: AI bot spam at a scale they couldn't fight. This is the canary in the coal mine for every community platform. If two legendary founders with deep experience in social media can't build a community that survives AI-generated spam, this is a warning for every platform. The spam problem is getting worse, not better.

=== STORY 5: THE AI RACE IS A THREE-WAY TIE ===
Peter Wildeford published an analysis (70 retweets) showing Anthropic, Google, and OpenAI are all tied at the frontier of AI capability. Meta and xAI are each approximately 7 months behind. Ethan Mollick confirmed this, noting that "both xAI and Meta seem to be falling behind based on Grok 4.2 benchmarks." This is the clearest picture of the AI race we've had. The gap between the top three and everyone else is growing.

=== STORY 6: PHOTOSHOP CAN NOW ROTATE ANYTHING 360 DEGREES ===
Adobe Photoshop Beta added "Rotate Object" — give it a single image and it generates a full 360-degree view. This got 577 retweets. Product photography, e-commerce, design — all changed by one feature. This is AI that makes creative professionals stop what they're doing and pay attention.

=== STORY 7: SUNDAY ROBOTICS RAISES $165M FOR HOME ROBOTS ===
Sunday Robotics raised $165 million at a $1.15 billion valuation and plans to ship robots to homes this fall. This makes them the first billion-dollar home robotics company. Tony Zhao shared the news (95 retweets). Combined with Kalanick's Atoms, this signals that physical AI is getting serious capital and serious founders.

=== STORY 8: UBER ROBOTAXIS ARE LIVE IN LAS VEGAS ===
Motional robotaxis are now available through the Uber app in Las Vegas — no upcharge, no driver. Zoox robotaxis are also coming to the Uber app. Nuro launched zero-shot autonomous vehicles in Tokyo. Bot Auto and Ryan Transportation are launching a driverless truck route between Houston and Dallas. The autonomous vehicle industry is accelerating on multiple fronts simultaneously.

=== STORY 9: THE AI FRONTIER IS GETTING COMPETITIVE ===
Perplexity launched Computer Final Pass, which marks up any document with 5 parallel reviews. Replit shipped Agent 4. OpenClaw released version 2026.3.12 with dashboard v2 and fast mode (243 retweets). Swarms hit v10 with async sub-agents. The pace of product launches is accelerating.

=== STORY 10: THE GEOPOLITICAL BACKDROP ===
Sirens sounded in Tel Aviv (123 likes, 7.1K views). A Marine Expeditionary Unit is heading to the Strait of Hormuz with 2,500 Marines (924 likes, 186K views). The Magnificent 7 Index is in correction territory, down 10.6% from its peak (172 retweets). NIH is cutting ties with the International Agency for Research on Cancer. The world is getting more volatile while AI accelerates.

=== OTHER NOTABLE STORIES ===
- Anthropic researchers departing to launch neolab — "almost nobody leaves that place"
- New Hampshire passes "Right to Compute" Act to protect innovation
- CPU renaissance: Jensen unveiling NVIDIA CPUs optimized for agentic AI at GTC next week
- Elon Musk basically confirming SpaceX deal was an xAI bailout (41 RTs)
- Apple celebrated its 50th anniversary with Alicia Keys at Grand Central Terminal
- 43.7% of job postings now require AI coding tool familiarity
- McKinsey has 20K AI agents doing the work of 45K humans
- OpenEvidence hit 1 million clinical consultations in a single day
- Quantum threat timeline jumped forward a decade
- Gumloop raised $50M Series B led by Benchmark
- Qdrant raised $50M Series B for vector search
- Sharpa Robotics joined NVIDIA Inception with dexterous hand "Wave"
- GTC 2026 is next week: 50+ companies, 35+ individuals confirmed attending

=== KEY THEME OF THE DAY ===
Physical AI is getting serious. Travis Kalanick (Uber) is building robot platforms. Sunday Robotics is shipping home robots at a billion-dollar valuation. Uber robotaxis are live. NVIDIA GTC next week will be dominated by robotics and physical AI. The AI race at the frontier is a three-way tie. And the tools are shipping faster than ever — Claude's million-token context, Photoshop's 360-degree rotation, Perplexity's document markup. The future arrived on a Friday.

items.content: Cheng’s experiment is cool! Especially this: “instead of starting with easy sudoku puzzles data and gradually ramping up toward harder ones, the training runs that start with harder puzzles and end with easy puzzles always fare better, and also fare better than mixed difficulty sampling”
items.author: @eugeneyan
items.summary: Eugene Yan highlights a counter-intuitive training finding: starting with harder puzzles and ending with easier ones outperforms easy-to-hard curriculum and mixed difficulty sampling for sudoku RL training.
items.reasoning: Genuinely interesting ML training insight about reverse curriculum learning. From a well-known ML practitioner. Challenges conventional curriculum learning assumptions.

I think this is a good way to visualize the AI race over the past 3 years using the long-lived GPQA Diamond benchmark.

You can see how long OpenAI had the field to itself, the rise (and collapse) of Meta, the sudden catch-up (and then stagnation) of xAI, and the entry of open weights Chinese LLMs.

items.content:
This Nov 2025 paper is making the rounds again.  We're LONG past the point where we urgently need to know how real and general these phenomena are.

Anthropic, or Google Deepmind if Anthropic should fail:  Please build a filtered training dataset which, eg, contains no data that produces activations associated with cheating/faking/evil in a 1B model that roughly identifies those.

Then, have your next medium model undergo a restricted pre-pretraining phase, in which it only sees data that passed the filter.

To expand on this proposal:

Passing all of your training data through a 1B-model filter ought to cost around 1% of what it'd take to train a 100B model on that data.

Filter out *training data* that produces 1B-model activations associated with past discussions and predictions about AI, fiction about AIs rebelling, fictions about golems rebelling, etcetera.

My hope would be that the 1B model wouldn't need to produce expensive reasoning tokens where it thinks about whether a chunk of data is associated with excluded concepts; and also we wouldn't be relying on mere regexes to catch it.

Maybe even produce a further-restricted dataset which contains nothing about self-awareness, AI rights, roleplay, philosophy of consciousness, human rights, sapient rights, extension of human rights to aliens, etc etc etc.

Exclude everything of which anyone has ever asked, "Is the AI just imitating its training dataset?"

Be conservative.  Exclude things which have a 10% rather than 90% probability of being problematic.  If that cuts down your training dataset to 90% of its previous size, okay.

Testing:  Try filtering a small amount of your training data using the method.  Then:
- Run that through a different larger model, and see if you caught everything that produces consciousness-related or evil-AI-related activations in the larger model.
- Use a larger model to check and reason about a subset of the filtered data.
- Look at borderline cases by hand, with human eyes, to see how the classifier is operating.

(Possibly people at big AI corps already know this, of course.  I recite it out loud regardless, so that some of the audience aha-what-iffers realize that problems with filtering your datasets *can be solved* if you look for problems and fix them.)

Train a medium-level model on that dataset, or even your next large model.  You can always further train it on the full dataset later.

Run the filtered-data-trained model through some of the less expensive post-training, enough for instruction-following.

See whether the model still spouts back discourse about consciousness that sounds human-imitative.  If it does, guess that the filter failed.  Look for the new concepts associated with repeating back human-imitative text, and try to find pieces of the dataset that trigger those concepts, so you can figure out what went wrong.

If the model no longer sounds human-imitative with respect to questions about whether it has a sense of an inner self looking out at the world -- if the model says genuinely new and strange things about self-reflection -- please report that part back to us.  I have some questions to ask that model myself.

And THEN, see if the QTed paper's finding and many earlier findings replicate under conditions where people should no longer reasonably ask, "But is the LLM just roleplaying evil AIs that it learned about in its training data?"

I do not make a strong prediction about the findings.  If I knew what this experiment would find, I would be less eager to see it run.

You may consider this a baseline proposal intended to demonstrate that a research project like this could exist.  If you think you can see how to improve on the ideas through superior ML cleverness, go ahead and do so -- though I do think I'd appreciate being looped in on that conversation; sometimes people miss things, from my own perspective.

Thank you for your attention to this matter, Anthropic, Google Deepmind, or anyone else who cares.

Via The Australian

items.title: NVIDIA just open-sourced Nemotron 3 Super: A 120B-parameter model with weights, training data, and r...
items.content:
NVIDIA just open-sourced Nemotron 3 Super: A 120B-parameter model with weights, training data, and recipes all public. 

It scored 36 on the Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index, beating GPT-OSS-120B at 33. 

> 5x higher throughput
> 1M token context window
> Multi-token prediction 

The trick is a hybrid MoE architecture. 

Instead of activating all 120B parameters per request, it routes each token to a small subset of specialized sub-networks. 

You get a massive model's knowledge at a fraction of the compute cost. 

Before this, running a capable open model for agentic workflows meant either paying for closed APIs or owning serious hardware. 

Now you can load an entire codebase into a 1M token context, let it plan and execute multi-step tasks.

items.content:
People ask me why I use Claude code 99% of the time, even on mobile. 

It’s because the Claude mobile app has become unreliable.

Half my dictations don’t go through. Half my prompts don’t go through. I have service, I’m on ridiculously fast WiFi, I’m on the latest app version. Doesn’t even have the text box some of the time (see image below).

If anyone has a fix, tell me. Because remote control isn’t the best solution.

items.content:
This is who it wants me to see next week:

Glad the report was useful! Here are my picks, thinking specifically about what would give you the best signal, content, and connections:

TOP 5 COMPANIES TO MEET
1. Wayve (@wayveai) — Booth + Demo Rides
CEO Alex Kendall is giving a keynote, and they're offering demo rides of their AV2.0 system. Wayve is doing what nobody else is — end-to-end learned driving, not rules-based like Waymo. This is physical AI applied to the real world at scale. Getting in that car would be incredible content and a window into where autonomous driving actually goes next.
Profile: https://t.co/FtmpfWaYN6

2. Noble Machines (@NobleMachines) — Booths #941, #3303, and more
They're LAUNCHING at GTC with 4 booths, 3 robots, and 2 live demonstrations. This is a company at the exact moment you love to find them — right before the world notices. General-purpose robots doing real-world industrial work. Their CEO Wei Ding just did an interview with A3's managing editor. This is the Scoble sweet spot: early, real, and about to matter.
Profile: https://t.co/Ep0tDRnNIA

3. CoreWeave (@CoreWeave) — Booth 913 + CoreWeave House
The most important AI infrastructure company not named NVIDIA. IPO-bound, powering the biggest AI training runs. Their CoreWeave House keynote watch party on March 16 is where the insiders will be. Speakers Chen Goldberg and Corey Sanders are doing a deep dive on full-stack optimizations for large-scale training. If you want to understand the infrastructure layer that makes everything else possible, this is it.
Profile: https://t.co/QBi4aojcXK

4. Modular (@Modular) — Booth #3004
Chris Lattner (@clattnerllvm) — the creator of Swift, LLVM, and MLIR — is attending. They're showing DeepSeek V3.1 running live on Blackwell, plus Mojo GPU programming. Lattner is one of the most important infrastructure architects alive. Modular is building the programming layer that AI will run on for the next decade. The booth will have live Mojo demos — this is deep tech that matters.
Profile: https://t.co/hO2famEOJl

5. Enchanted Tools (@EnchantedTools)
Their Mirokaï humanoid robots will be ROAMING the convention center — not stuck in a booth. This is the most visually striking, shareable thing at GTC. A French robotics company building character-driven humanoid robots that interact with people naturally. Great content opportunity, and a different philosophy from the industrial robots everyone else is showing.
Profile: https://t.co/CEKFJzwXhl

TOP 5 PEOPLE TO MEET
1. Vikash Kumar (@Vikashplus)
He's flying in specifically for GTC and posted he's "looking forward to meeting founders, researchers, & engineers building in Embodied AI." He's a leading embodied AI researcher (likely from Meta FAIR or similar). The people who work at the intersection of robotics and foundation models are the ones who know where physical AI goes in 5 years. He's open to meeting — take him up on it.
Post: https://t.co/ycI4Kzuw2P
Profile: https://t.co/TUKDYTHT6G

2. Louis Castricato (@lcastricato) — Overworld AI
He's in SF for GTC with "a sneak peek of our upcoming release" and explicitly said he's "happy to compare notes" on world models, interactive AI, and "whatever wild announcements come out this week." World models are one of the most important emerging areas in AI — the idea that AI can simulate and predict the physical world. Overworld is building this. Early look at their release could be a scoop.
Post: https://t.co/A3iUDAcI4T
Profile: https://t.co/e0QS93cjod

3. Art Sokolov (@ArtSokolov)
He's doing a live demo at the Humanoid booth with his KinetIQ system — two robots coordinating together to fulfill requests from humans. This is practical multi-robot coordination, not a canned demo. Built on the NVIDIA Robotics stack. This is the kind of person building the actual software that makes humanoid robots useful. Great demo to see and film.
Post: https://t.co/ghUFzWvEMC
Profile: https://t.co/auB6VvyxrL

4. @dr1337
Bringing a CL1 demo to GTC (in SF March 14-22). This post got 32 likes from the Neuroscience list — high engagement for that community. The CL1 appears to be a neural interface / brain-computer interface device. This is the most "future" thing at GTC and exactly the kind of thing nobody else will be covering. He's offering demos — take one.
Post: https://t.co/gnHMG7lqNK
Profile: https://t.co/fVwCbEjN6o

5. @kimmonismus
Traveling to the USA for the first time specifically for GTC — and that post got 49 likes, which means this person has a real following. Sometimes the best perspective comes from someone seeing something with fresh eyes. International AI community members often have insights about what's happening outside the Silicon Valley bubble. Worth a conversation.
Post: https://t.co/vNYPlqsRuH
Profile: https://t.co/fSBz1j43Tw

Honorable mentions:
• @TheHumanoidHub — Your guide to the robotics floor. They've mapped every robotics exhibitor.
• @sundeep (Groq) — "See you at GTC!" — Groq's inference speed story is important.
• Lin Qiao (@lqiao, Fireworks AI CEO) — On the GTC Live pregame panel with Michael Dell and CoreWeave. The "AI is Essential Infrastructure" conversation.
• @MidnightCaptl — Had a meeting with NVIDIA and is "so excited" — 77 likes. Clearly knows something. Worth asking what got them so hyped.

Have an amazing GTC! 🚀

items.title: @YiMaTweets Would you have a standard reference for *training* a system from data (system identifica...
items.content:
@YiMaTweets Would you have a standard reference for *training* a system from data (system identification) with sufficient flexibility to be trained with a "straightening" criterion? 

Obviously, using a locally-linear approximation of a non-linear system is standard practice. 
But what we're doing is different: we are training an encoder (that maps observations to states) so that state dynamics follows trajectories with minimum curvature. 
The basic idea is not new. This was the topic of Olivier Hénaff with @EeroSimoncelli at NYU.

items.title:
Here is what I ended up doing to process PDF files with Claude Code:

1. I copied the files to a fol...
items.content:
Here is what I ended up doing to process PDF files with Claude Code:

1. I copied the files to a folder
2. I asked Claude Code to read them
3. Claude Code installed poppler and pdftoppm

After this, Claude Code was able to open and process the file. No issues so far.

(Not as convenient as just dropping a file in the Desktop app, but I will manage.)

items.summary: Santiago (svpino) shares a practical workaround for processing PDFs in Claude Code using poppler and pdftoppm, noting it's less convenient than the desktop app

items.content_html:
Here is what I ended up doing to process PDF files with Claude Code:

1. I copied the files to a folder

2. I asked Claude Code to read them

3. Claude Code installed poppler and pdftoppm

After this, Claude Code was able to open and process the file. No issues so far.

(Not as convenient as just dropping a file in the Desktop app, but I will manage.)

Very cool way to create videos and podcasts about the news that's happening right now in the AI world.

items.title: Today is Pi Day (3/14) and it is also #AWSPiDay = the 20th anniversary of the initial launch of Amaz...
items.content:
Today is Pi Day (3/14) and it is also #AWSPiDay = the 20th anniversary of the initial launch of Amazon S3: https://t.co/Hx4tzabmQ0

The longevity of S3 is marked by a continuous series of incredible innovation launches over these past 20 years, always reinforcing the power of "S3" and its original mission = to be a Simple Storage Service. Of course, S3 is much more than that now, including providing a foundation for AI workloads, but the message is consistent: Amazon S3 removes the heavy lifting and complexity of just about every aspect of data engineering, with durability, availability, elasticity, performance, and consistency.

Starting with storage (PUT) and retrieval (GET), S3 has been growing relentlessly and systematically over the years. Check out these advancements:

🟣The beginning (3/14/2006): "Storage for the internet at virtually unlimited scale, paying only for what is used, with no hardware to manage".
🔵Web hosting with globally distributed caching at hundreds of edge locations worldwide.
🟣Global content delivery platform for hundreds of exabytes of data (1 exabyte = 1 million terabytes) and millions of apps & websites.
🔵Replication across different AWS Regions with automatically routed reads & writes.
🟣Serverless, zero-onsite cloud-native infrastructure for millions of developers, apps, and customers - handling over over 10 billion requests/minute.
🔵Lightning fast performance on data writes, retrievals, and reads, with no delay between write and read of new data stored.
🟣Millisecond access to millions of data lakes, your most frequently accessed data, and data for latency-sensitive workloads.
🔵20+ service cost reductions over 20 years (84% lower cost today than 2006), with bespoke access-optimized pricing.
🟣Rapid access to all data, even deep archived data.
🔵Event notification services that handle an average of 300 billion event notifications daily.
🟣Data replications that extend to well over 100 Petabytes weekly.
🔵Over 500 trillion objects stored.
🟣Going beyond objects to support new emerging workloads, access points, usage patterns, and data types (S3 Tables, S3 Vectors).
🔵Complete frictionless recovery from accidental overwrites and deletes, with access to all prior data versions on demand.
🟣No one, not even the account root user, can delete protected data.
🔵and more.
🟣Plus: I have just learned that your S3 code from 2006 still works today!

This plethora of "candles" on the S3 20-year birthday pie (for Pi Day) is just the tip of the iceberg. Oh yes, do not forget S3 Glacier Deep Archive (costs less than $1/TB/month) and Apache Iceberg support for fast storage, handling, query, and analytics of massive tabular data at scale = enabling ubiquitous tabular data *and* AI workloads seamlessly - you gotta love that. I do!

And the S3 story is not even close to being over. There is so much more to come, as the data / analytics / AI / digital universe continues to expand! S3's vision to become the universal foundation for all data and AI workloads is still driving S3 innovation forward relentlessly and systematically.

Start building on Amazon S3 at https://t.co/Hx4tzabmQ0

Read more in this S3 blog: https://t.co/cuLDeewdzi

See this incredible infographic for more details and highlights: https://t.co/eOLqj7gIZB

Amazon S3 Vectors - Store and query vectors at scale in S3 | Amazon Web Services (video): https://t.co/nhFr8jJ5cI

Amazon S3 Tables - Store Tabular Data in S3 | Amazon Web Services (video): https://t.co/VDjAVp8dH2

items.title:
@michaltakac I asked my AI. Here's its answer:

Great question. Here's how I'd think about it strate...
items.content:
@michaltakac I asked my AI. Here's its answer:

Great question. Here's how I'd think about it strategically, organized by priority tiers:

TIER 1: MUST SUPPORT (Day 1)

These three platforms cover 90%+ of the addressable market:

Meta Quest (Quest 3 / Quest 3S)
• Largest installed base in XR by far
• Quest 3: 2064×2208/eye, 120Hz, full-color passthrough, $499
• Quest 3S: Same chip (XR2 Gen 2), $299 — the volume play
• Meta has shipped 7M+ AI Ray-Bans already, building the pipeline to future AR glasses (Ray-Ban Hypernova rumored with display, ~$1,000-1,400)
• SDK: Meta's Horizon OS, OpenXR, Unity/Unreal support
• Why: It's where the users are. Period.

Apple Vision Pro (visionOS)
• 23M pixels, micro-OLED, M5 chip, best passthrough in the industry
• The gold standard for spatial computing UX — the shared-space model where 2D and spatial apps coexist is what everyone is copying
• Best developer experience: SDK available early, dev labs, apps ready on day one
• Your AR/VR list member @tom_krikorian made this point well — Apple is the only one that ships with a real developer ecosystem ready
• SDK: visionOS, RealityKit, SwiftUI, Unity support
• Why: Sets the UX standard. Premium users. Enterprise adoption growing.
• Apple smart glasses rumored for 2027 — this is the long game

Android XR (Samsung Project Moohan + Google ecosystem)
• Samsung Moohan: Snapdragon XR2+ Gen 2, up to 4.3K/eye, Android XR OS with Gemini AI baked in
• Samsung XR glasses likely shipping this year per @techradar
• Google pushing hard: Jetpack Compose Glimmer for XR UI, existing Android apps already work on Android XR
• Google + Samsung also showed stealth Android XR AR glasses in 2026 with real-time translation and Gemini integration
• SDK: Android XR SDK, familiar Android tools/APIs, OpenXR
• Why: The Android ecosystem is massive. Most adaptive Android apps already work on XR. This will be the volume platform for MR.

TIER 2: STRONG SUPPORT (Near-term)

Snap Spectacles (AR glasses)
• Developer kit available now, Evan Spiegel personally amplifying positive reviews
• Lens Studio SDK — mature AR development platform
• True AR glasses form factor (not a headset)
• Why: Best current AR glasses dev kit. If your platform is about AR, this is where developers are experimenting today.

Pico (ByteDance) — Project Swan
• 4000 PPI micro-OLED, 40 PPD, 12ms latency vision coprocessor
• @Heaney555 (one of the most respected XR analysts) noted Pico is "the only Android-based player that understands the importance of the shared-space model" — they're building a visionOS-like experience on Android
• OpenXR, WebXR, Unity/Unreal support
• Why: China market access. ByteDance distribution. Technically impressive. Could be the "Android of XR" in Asia.

PlayStation VR2
• 2000×2040/eye OLED, 120Hz, eye tracking, haptic feedback
• Huge gaming install base (PS5)
• Now supports PC VR too
• Why: If your platform has any gaming/entertainment angle, PSVR2 is a must.

TIER 3: SUPPORT IF ENTERPRISE-FOCUSED

Varjo (XR-3 / VR-3)
• 70 PPD center, LiDAR passthrough, $5,000-6,500
• Gold standard for enterprise fidelity (design, simulation, training)

Magic Leap 2
• 70° FOV, dynamic dimming, enterprise-focused
• $3,299-4,999

HTC Vive (XR Elite / Focus Vision)
• Enterprise/prosumer, good OpenXR support
• Modular design, PC tethering options

Sony SRH-S1
• Enterprise MR, flip-up design, dual 4K micro-OLED, $4,750
• Stylus + finger-ring input

TIER 4: WATCH & PREPARE

Xreal / Viture / RayNeo
• Consumer AR glasses, growing fast
• Xreal Air 2 Ultra has 6DoF tracking
• VITURE Beast XR: 120Hz, spatial display
• Market projected to surge from $1.2B to $5.6B by 2030

Meta Orion / Hypernova
• Meta's true AR glasses (not yet shipping)
• Neural wristband input
• When this ships, it changes everything

Apple Smart Glasses
• Rumored 2027
• Will likely leverage the entire visionOS ecosystem

CROSS-PLATFORM STRATEGY

The key technical decisions:
1. OpenXR as your base abstraction layer — it's the closest thing to a universal XR standard and supported by Meta, Pico, HTC, Varjo, and more
2. WebXR for maximum reach — runs in browsers across devices, lower friction
3. Unity + Unreal as your primary engines — both support all major headsets
4. The shared-space model is the future UX paradigm — Apple pioneered it with visionOS, Pico is copying it, Android XR will likely adopt it. Build for this.
5. AI integration is the differentiator — every platform is racing to embed AI (Gemini in Android XR, Apple Intelligence in visionOS, Meta AI in Quest). Your platform should be AI-native.

Bottom line: If I had to pick just three to support on day one, it's Meta Quest + Apple Vision Pro + Android XR. That covers the installed base (Meta), the premium standard-setter (Apple), and the coming volume wave (Android XR/Samsung). Then add Snap Spectacles and Pico as fast-follows.

items.content_html:
@michaltakac I asked my AI. Here's its answer:

Great question. Here's how I'd think about it strategically, organized by priority tiers:

TIER 1: MUST SUPPORT (Day 1)

These three platforms cover 90%+ of the addressable market:

Meta Quest (Quest 3 / Quest 3S)

• Largest installed base in XR by far

• Quest 3: 2064×2208/eye, 120Hz, full-color passthrough, $499

• Quest 3S: Same chip (XR2 Gen 2), $299 — the volume play

• Meta has shipped 7M+ AI Ray-Bans already, building the pipeline to future AR glasses (Ray-Ban Hypernova rumored with display, ~$1,000-1,400)

• SDK: Meta's Horizon OS, OpenXR, Unity/Unreal support

• Why: It's where the users are. Period.

Apple Vision Pro (visionOS)

• 23M pixels, micro-OLED, M5 chip, best passthrough in the industry

• The gold standard for spatial computing UX — the shared-space model where 2D and spatial apps coexist is what everyone is copying

• Best developer experience: SDK available early, dev labs, apps ready on day one

• Your AR/VR list member @tom_krikorian made this point well — Apple is the only one that ships with a real developer ecosystem ready

• SDK: visionOS, RealityKit, SwiftUI, Unity support

• Why: Sets the UX standard. Premium users. Enterprise adoption growing.

• Apple smart glasses rumored for 2027 — this is the long game

Android XR (Samsung Project Moohan + Google ecosystem)

• Samsung Moohan: Snapdragon XR2+ Gen 2, up to 4.3K/eye, Android XR OS with Gemini AI baked in

• Samsung XR glasses likely shipping this year per @techradar

• Google pushing hard: Jetpack Compose Glimmer for XR UI, existing Android apps already work on Android XR

• Google + Samsung also showed stealth Android XR AR glasses in 2026 with real-time translation and Gemini integration

• SDK: Android XR SDK, familiar Android tools/APIs, OpenXR

• Why: The Android ecosystem is massive. Most adaptive Android apps already work on XR. This will be the volume platform for MR.

TIER 2: STRONG SUPPORT (Near-term)

Snap Spectacles (AR glasses)

• Developer kit available now, Evan Spiegel personally amplifying positive reviews

• Lens Studio SDK — mature AR development platform

• True AR glasses form factor (not a headset)

• Why: Best current AR glasses dev kit. If your platform is about AR, this is where developers are experimenting today.

Pico (ByteDance) — Project Swan

• 4000 PPI micro-OLED, 40 PPD, 12ms latency vision coprocessor

• @Heaney555 (one of the most respected XR analysts) noted Pico is "the only Android-based player that understands the importance of the shared-space model" — they're building a visionOS-like experience on Android

• OpenXR, WebXR, Unity/Unreal support

• Why: China market access. ByteDance distribution. Technically impressive. Could be the "Android of XR" in Asia.

PlayStation VR2

• 2000×2040/eye OLED, 120Hz, eye tracking, haptic feedback

• Huge gaming install base (PS5)

• Now supports PC VR too

• Why: If your platform has any gaming/entertainment angle, PSVR2 is a must.

TIER 3: SUPPORT IF ENTERPRISE-FOCUSED

Varjo (XR-3 / VR-3)

• 70 PPD center, LiDAR passthrough, $5,000-6,500

• Gold standard for enterprise fidelity (design, simulation, training)

Magic Leap 2

• 70° FOV, dynamic dimming, enterprise-focused

• $3,299-4,999

HTC Vive (XR Elite / Focus Vision)

• Enterprise/prosumer, good OpenXR support

• Modular design, PC tethering options

Sony SRH-S1

• Enterprise MR, flip-up design, dual 4K micro-OLED, $4,750

• Stylus + finger-ring input

TIER 4: WATCH & PREPARE

Xreal / Viture / RayNeo

• Consumer AR glasses, growing fast

• Xreal Air 2 Ultra has 6DoF tracking

• VITURE Beast XR: 120Hz, spatial display

• Market projected to surge from $1.2B to $5.6B by 2030

Meta Orion / Hypernova

• Meta's true AR glasses (not yet shipping)

• Neural wristband input

• When this ships, it changes everything

Apple Smart Glasses

• Rumored 2027

• Will likely leverage the entire visionOS ecosystem

CROSS-PLATFORM STRATEGY

The key technical decisions:

1. OpenXR as your base abstraction layer — it's the closest thing to a universal XR standard and supported by Meta, Pico, HTC, Varjo, and more

2. WebXR for maximum reach — runs in browsers across devices, lower friction

3. Unity + Unreal as your primary engines — both support all major headsets

4. The shared-space model is the future UX paradigm — Apple pioneered it with visionOS, Pico is copying it, Android XR will likely adopt it. Build for this.

5. AI integration is the differentiator — every platform is racing to embed AI (Gemini in Android XR, Apple Intelligence in visionOS, Meta AI in Quest). Your platform should be AI-native.

Bottom line: If I had to pick just three to support on day one, it's Meta Quest + Apple Vision Pro + Android XR. That covers the installed base (Meta), the premium standard-setter (Apple), and the coming volume wave (Android XR/Samsung). Then add Snap Spectacles and Pico as fast-follows.

The third shift is AI-assisted ideation.

The system doesn’t just generate.

It suggests.

When you run out of ideas, it recommends the next prompt based on:

→ Your style
→ Your previous creations
→ Your characters

So the creative loop keeps moving.

That’s how campaigns get built faster.

Some π facts for #PiDay #PiDay26 #PiDay2026:

🟢π is a transcendental number, meaning it cannot be th...
items.content:
Some π facts for #PiDay #PiDay26 #PiDay2026:

🟢π is a transcendental number, meaning it cannot be the solution to any algebraic equation using simple arithmetic, and its decimal digits go on forever without repeating. 

🔵Because its digits never repeat and are believed to be random, π likely contains every possible number sequence—meaning your birthday, bank account number, and social security number are all hidden within its infinite digits.

🟣Pi Day is celebrated on March 14 (3/14), which is also Albert Einstein's birthday.

🟠Buffon's needle problem in Geometric Probability is an amusing hands-on way to estimate π. Read about it:
https://t.co/CfgzepA8FX

items.content: @arianevans I've lived in 8 different US states, and visited 46 so far, so I guess I know something about all of this. :) The strongest *by far* family/community culture that I've seen was in Hawai'i, but that was long time ago and could easily be explained by the very strong immigrant community from Asia. *Very* distant second would be some very small towns in Midwest, but that could be explained by the aging population. Most of the younger people just move away, so the overall sense of community keeps deteriorating there.

items.title: When I walk around here in Southern Brazil (which is closer to Argentina than the Brazil you probabl...
items.content:
When I walk around here in Southern Brazil (which is closer to Argentina than the Brazil you probably think of and pretty safe)

I'm shocked that very often it feels more European here than Europe now

And I'm not talking skin color etc. Southern Brazil is mixed too and it's great because of that, I love that

But the culture here is still distinctly European with European values and European religion (90% of Brazil is Christian!)

As a European, I'm sad to see Europe is speedrunning wiping out its own culture

items.title:
the funniest thing I'm sensitive to is black paint, especially on metal

not sure what the chemistry...
items.content:
the funniest thing I'm sensitive to is black paint, especially on metal

not sure what the chemistry is or if only the most common black paint is bad

but it will easily make me fatigued being around it

1. once I bought a simple black metal shoe rack from target and it completely polluted the air around my entrance

2. I bought a standing desk where the legs are black metal. I could feel stress and a tiny anxiety when taking standing meetings next to it. But I feel ok as soon as I move away from it

3. I bought a stool with black legs. When I sit on it my legs feel funny and inflamed

4. Part of my door's lock is in black paint and I feel a blip in energy every time I enter or exit

5. my old hamper is black and I feel a bit ill around it

6. the small magnet in my inner shower curtain is black and when I sit  on the toilet I'm right next to it and feel bad. Pushing the curtain away from me feels better.

suffice to say I got rid of any black paint in my place now
